A Brand Identity Concept — Bennachie Coffee

The Highlands deserve
a mark to match.

Artisan Coffee · Aberdeenshire, Scotland

The Logomark

BC Monogram — Four Expressions

Primary — Dark Ground
Reversed — Light Ground
Heritage — Mid Tone
Premium — Gold Ground
The Opportunity

The coffee is exceptional.
The brand should say so.

Bennachie Coffee has a name rooted in Scottish heritage, a compelling origin story, and a monogram with genuine character. What it does not yet have is a brand identity that carries the same weight as the product in the cup. That gap is exactly where Brand Architect works.

The message does not match the ambition

The current positioning undersells what Bennachie Coffee is. A brand with this name, this story, and this product has the credentials to command a premium audience — and the identity should reflect that from the very first word.

The typography is not working hard enough

The right typeface does not merely present words — it communicates standing, authority, and character before a single sentence is read. Typography is one of the most powerful tools a premium brand possesses. Right now, that tool is sitting unused.

There is no unified visual voice

A brand at this level speaks consistently across every touchpoint — packaging, digital, print, social. What exists today is a collection of elements rather than a system. The difference between the two is the difference between a business and a brand.

The story deserves a bigger stage

From a spark of inspiration in Sydney to a specialty roaster bearing the name of an Aberdeenshire landmark — that is a story worth telling boldly. Heritage, provenance, and passion are among the most valuable assets a premium brand can own.

The monogram deserves a system around it

The BC mark has real presence. But a logomark only reaches its full potential when it sits within a considered identity system — one where colour, type, layout, and tone all pull in the same direction. That is what turns a logo into a brand.

Premium signals are being left on the table

Every detail of a brand communicates value — or fails to. The brands that command loyalty and price premiums in the specialty coffee market are not simply better coffee. They are better brands. Bennachie Coffee has every ingredient to be among them.

Proposed Palette

Colour that carries character.

Colour is not decoration — it is identity. A considered palette applied with consistency becomes as recognisable as the mark itself.

Espresso #1A1208
Roast #3A2C14
Antique Gold #8B6420
Parchment #D4B896
Highland Mist #F2F2F2
Typography

Typography that earns its place.

The right typefaces do not simply present words — they signal standing. In the specialty food and drink market, typography is one of the clearest indicators of whether a brand belongs at the premium tier. It is a detail that customers feel before they consciously notice it.

Display Serif — crafted
for distinction
From the Hills of Aberdeenshire.
Subheading Serif — authority
without weight
Specialty-grade. Ethically sourced. Roasted in Scotland.
Body Sans — clean,
considered, modern
Ross Erskine began envisioning Bennachie Coffee after experiencing Sydney's exceptional coffee culture in 2001. Sixteen years of refinement followed before the first bag was roasted.
Label / UI Sans — precision
at every scale
Highland Blend · Single Origin · Specialty Grade
The Opportunity

"The monogram alone is worth protecting. Paired with the right identity system, Bennachie Coffee stops looking like a craft roaster and starts looking like a highland institution."

— Brand Architect
Next Step

This is only the beginning, Ross.

What you see here is a speculative glimpse — a direction, not a destination. The full Brand Architect identity package encompasses twelve artboards: brand heart, primary logomark, all variations, colour and typography systems, digital and physical applications. Everything Bennachie Coffee needs to carry the weight of its name.
